heroOverview: Choroideremia is an X-linked inherited retinal dystrophy caused by loss-of-function variants in the CHM gene, which encodes Rab escort protein-1 (REP-1). Without functional REP-1, retinal pigment epithelium cells, photoreceptors, and choroidal capillaries progressively degenerate in a centrifugal pattern, sparing central vision until later decades. The condition affects approximately 1 in 50,000 individuals and accounts for roughly 4% of all blindness in school-age males. Because the causal gene sits on the X chromosome, males are fully affected while females are typically carriers who may have subtle fundus changes but retain near-normal visual function. Night blindness in childhood is the hallmark first symptom, followed by a slowly contracting visual field that advances for decades before central acuity is lost. Choroideremia is one of the most genetically defined retinal dystrophies and is currently the subject of multiple gene therapy clinical trials.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1 in 50,000 individuals worldwide; accounts for about 4% of blindness in school-age males (Foundation Fighting Blindness)
keyStats.demographics: Almost exclusively affects males (X-linked recessive); female carriers rarely develop significant visual impairment
keyStats.avgAge: Night blindness typically begins in the first to second decade; significant visual field loss by the third to fifth decade; central vision loss often in the fifth to seventh decade

definition: Choroideremia (ICD-10: H31.21) is an X-linked recessive chorioretinal dystrophy caused by hemizygous mutations in the CHM gene on chromosome Xq21.2, encoding Rab escort protein-1 (REP-1). REP-1 is required for the prenylation of Rab GTPases, a family of proteins that regulate intracellular vesicle trafficking in all tissues. In the retina, impaired vesicle trafficking disrupts melanosome processing in the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE), outer segment renewal in photoreceptors, and maintenance of the choriocapillaris, all of which depend on precise membrane trafficking for survival. Degeneration begins peripherally and advances centrally over decades, sparing the fovea until relatively late in the disease course. Fundoscopy in affected males shows a characteristic scalloped pattern of RPE and choroidal atrophy that is highly distinctive; with disease progression, the underlying sclera becomes visible as white patches with islands of preserved retina around the posterior pole. Carrier females show patchy midperipheral RPE changes without significant visual impairment in the majority of cases, though approximately 5% develop symptomatic peripheral visual field loss. Choroideremia falls within the inherited retinal dystrophies cared for by vitreoretinal and retinal specialist ophthalmologists. It is clinically and genetically distinct from retinitis pigmentosa — with which it is most commonly confused — by its X-linked inheritance, distinctive fundus appearance, and preservation of the RPE-Bruch membrane complex until later stages.

typesClassification.description: Full phenotype with progressive night blindness, concentric visual field loss, and eventual central visual impairment; caused by null or severe missense CHM mutations.
Patchy midperipheral RPE and choroidal atrophy visible on fundoscopy; most carriers are visually asymptomatic, though approximately 5% develop meaningful peripheral field loss in later decades.
Rare cases with unusually mild progression in hemizygous males, sometimes associated with hypomorphic CHM variants; central vision may be preserved into the seventh decade.

primarySymptoms: Night blindness (nyctalopia) — difficulty seeing in dim light or adapting from bright to dark environments; typically the earliest and most consistent symptom, appearing in the first or second decade of life.
Progressive loss of peripheral vision — a gradually contracting visual field that begins in the mid-periphery and advances concentrically, described as looking through an ever-narrowing tunnel.
Difficulty navigating in low-light environments such as unlit corridors, cinemas, and outdoor spaces at dusk — a direct consequence of rod photoreceptor loss in the retinal periphery.
Glare sensitivity and prolonged dark adaptation time — affected individuals take longer than normal to adjust when moving from a bright to a dark room.
Loss of peripheral awareness causing bumps and near-misses with objects at the sides, particularly in crowded environments; this is a major safety concern that worsens over decades.
Reduced contrast sensitivity, which affects reading and face recognition before formal visual acuity declines significantly.
Eventual difficulty with reading and fine visual tasks as the central foveal island of preserved vision narrows in later disease stages.
Photopsia — brief flashes of light in the peripheral visual field, which occur as degenerating photoreceptors discharge spontaneously.
Impaired colour discrimination in later stages when foveal cones are affected, though colour vision is typically well preserved during the first four decades.
Carrier females may notice mild night vision difficulties, subtle midperipheral scotomata on formal testing, or occasional photopsia, though most have no functional visual complaint.
earlyWarningSigns: A boy or adolescent male who struggles to see or becomes cautious or hesitant in dim lighting, or who avoids activities at dusk.
Recurrent trips or collisions with peripheral objects in otherwise healthy vision — suggests peripheral field loss rather than a central visual problem.
A fundus examination showing patchy midperipheral RPE atrophy in a male with a family history of blindness in maternal relatives.
A mother (obligate carrier) whose ophthalmoscopy shows characteristic midperipheral choroidal atrophy — a prompt to screen male offspring.
emergencySigns: Sudden loss of central vision in a previously affected eye — while choroideremia progresses slowly, abrupt central visual loss can indicate a complicating choroidal neovascular membrane, which requires urgent assessment and possible anti-VEGF treatment.
Sudden unilateral loss of any remaining visual field, suggesting retinal detachment — a recognised though uncommon complication of end-stage choroidal atrophy.
New flashes or floaters in a patient who has undergone or is being considered for subretinal gene therapy — these may indicate procedural complications.

causes.cause: Hemizygous loss-of-function mutations in CHM gene (Xq21.2)
causes.description: The vast majority of choroideremia cases are caused by nonsense mutations, frameshift insertions or deletions, splice-site changes, or large chromosomal deletions in CHM that eliminate functional REP-1 protein, preventing proper Rab GTPase prenylation and disrupting vesicle trafficking in RPE cells, photoreceptors, and choroidal endothelial cells.

causes.cause: Impaired retinal pigment epithelium vesicle trafficking
causes.description: Loss of REP-1 in the RPE disrupts melanosome biogenesis and phagocytosis of shed photoreceptor outer segments, leading to RPE cell death; as RPE cells die, the overlying photoreceptors they support lose their nutritional and recycling supply and undergo secondary degeneration.

REP-1 deficiency in choroidal endothelial cells impairs vesicle membrane trafficking required for capillary maintenance; choriocapillary loss restricts the metabolic supply to both RPE and photoreceptors, accelerating the degenerative cascade from the outer retina inward.

Rod and cone photoreceptors continuously renew their outer segments, a process that depends on RPE phagocytosis and precise membrane recycling via Rab GTPases; REP-1 deficiency impairs this renewal, causing progressive outer segment shortening and eventual photoreceptor death beginning in the rod-rich midperiphery.

Approximately 5-10% of choroideremia cases arise from de novo mutations in CHM with no prior family history of the condition, which is important for genetic counselling as affected males may have apparently unaffected mothers who nonetheless carry the mutation or are de novo carriers.

riskFactors.description: Males carry a single X chromosome and therefore express the full choroideremia phenotype when the CHM mutation is present; females with one mutant and one normal X chromosome are typically carriers with minimal functional impairment.

riskFactors.factor: Positive family history on the maternal line
riskFactors.description: X-linked inheritance means the condition passes through carrier mothers; a mother with an affected brother, father, or maternal uncle has a 50% chance of transmitting the mutation to each of her sons.

riskFactors.factor: Carrier female status with additional risk factors for progression
riskFactors.description: The approximately 5% of carrier females who develop meaningful visual impairment tend to have more extensive fundus changes on OCT and electroretinography; skewed X-inactivation toward the affected allele is the probable mechanism.

Null mutations (nonsense, frameshift, large deletion) are associated with earlier and more severe disease onset than missense variants; genotype-phenotype correlations are imprecise, but mutation class informs prognosis in clinical gene therapy trials.
The rare contiguous gene deletion syndrome (Xq21 deletion) causes choroideremia together with mental retardation and deafness; isolated CHM deletion does not carry systemic associations.

affectedDemographics: Hemizygous males — the primary affected population, with disease onset in childhood and progressive visual loss over decades
Carrier females — rarely symptomatic but may have mild peripheral field changes detectable on formal testing
Boys and adolescent males, in whom night blindness and peripheral vision difficulties are the first presenting features
Families with a history of X-linked inherited visual impairment, particularly where multiple maternal-line males are affected
Individuals of any ethnicity — no specific geographic or racial predilection has been established
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of choroideremia requires a combination of clinical history, fundoscopy, electrophysiology, multimodal retinal imaging, and genetic testing. The characteristic fundus appearance — peripheral scalloped chorioretinal atrophy with a sharp border preserving the posterior pole — is usually sufficient for a clinical diagnosis in an affected male with a consistent history. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) maps the remaining ellipsoid zone (EZ) area, which correlates directly with best-corrected visual acuity and is the primary structural outcome measure in clinical trials. Full-field electroretinography (ERG) demonstrates severe rod system dysfunction with preserved or relatively preserved cone responses in early to mid-stage disease, reflecting the centrifugal degeneration pattern. Fundus autofluorescence (FAF) reveals the sharp boundary between degenerated and preserved retinal tissue with great sensitivity, and microperimetry precisely maps residual visual function within the preserved central island. Carrier detection in females is performed by fundus examination (showing the characteristic patchy midperipheral changes), FAF, and genetic testing. Genetic confirmation with CHM gene sequencing and deletion/duplication analysis is essential for establishing the molecular diagnosis, providing accurate prognosis, enabling genetic counselling, and determining eligibility for gene therapy clinical trials. Differential diagnosis from retinitis pigmentosa is critical: the X-linked inheritance in choroideremia, the sharp scalloped border on FAF, and the absence of bone-spicule pigmentation (which is characteristic of RP) are the key distinguishing features. Gyrate atrophy (caused by OAT gene mutations, autosomal recessive) is another close differential, differentiated by elevated plasma ornithine levels.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly visualises the characteristic peripheral scalloped chorioretinal atrophy; in advanced disease shows white sclera through the affected zones with sparing of the posterior pole.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Eye drops widen your pupils over 20-30 minutes; the ophthalmologist then shines a light into each eye and uses a lens to examine the retina. It takes about 15 minutes and your near vision will be blurred for a few hours after.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures the area of preserved ellipsoid zone (photoreceptor inner-outer segment junction), which is the primary marker of remaining functional retina and the main outcome in gene therapy trials.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A camera scans the back of your eye with harmless infrared light; no contact is needed. The scan takes about five minutes and produces a detailed cross-sectional map of the retinal layers.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Highlights the sharp demarcation between degenerated and surviving RPE cells; the retained autofluorescence of intact RPE lipofuscin creates a bright ring around the preserved central island.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fundus camera with a blue or green excitation light takes wide-angle photographs of the retina after pupil dilation; it is painless and takes 5-10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures the electrical responses of rods and cones to flashing light stimuli; demonstrates markedly reduced or absent rod responses with preserved cone responses in early disease, confirming the diagnosis and staging disease severity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small contact lens electrode is placed on the numbed eye surface and you watch a series of flashing lights in the dark and then in bright conditions; the test takes about 45-60 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps the spatial distribution of remaining visual sensitivity, quantifies the area of residual central vision, and tracks progressive field loss over time as an outcome measure.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You press a button whenever you see dim lights appear at different positions on a screen while looking at a central target; the test takes 15-30 minutes per eye.

diagnosticTests.test: CHM gene sequencing and deletion/duplication analysis
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative mutation for definitive diagnosis, carrier testing of female relatives, prenatal or preimplantation genetic diagnosis, and determination of gene therapy trial eligibility.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample is sent for comprehensive DNA analysis of the CHM gene; results typically take four to eight weeks from a specialist laboratory.

treatmentOverview: There is currently no approved treatment that halts or reverses the degeneration in choroideremia, but the disease is the subject of advanced gene therapy clinical programmes that are the most promising avenue to date. Subretinal injection of adeno-associated virus serotype 2 (AAV2) carrying a functional copy of CHM has been evaluated in Phase I and II trials; early-phase data showed safety and some evidence of preserved visual function in treated eyes compared with controls, though Phase III trials have produced more mixed results, highlighting the difficulty of measuring meaningful benefit in a slowly progressive disease. Multiple next-generation gene therapy approaches using modified AAV serotypes and improved promoters are in active clinical trials as of 2025. Outside gene therapy, management is supportive and includes regular retinal surveillance with OCT and microperimetry to track progression, low vision aids and rehabilitation as central vision declines, genetic counselling for affected individuals and their carrier relatives, and psychological and peer support through patient organisations. Choroidal neovascular membranes — a complication in a minority of patients — are treated with anti-VEGF intravitreal injections. Cataract, which occurs earlier in some patients with advanced disease, is managed surgically with appropriate counselling about visual prognosis given the underlying retinal degeneration.

medicalTreatments.name: AAV2-REP1 subretinal gene therapy (clinical trials)
medicalTreatments.description: Adeno-associated virus vector carrying a full-length functional CHM complementary DNA is injected into the subretinal space under general or local anaesthesia; the intention is to restore REP-1 expression in surviving RPE cells and photoreceptors, halting further degeneration. Ongoing Phase II and III trials are refining the optimal stage of disease and vector dose.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Phase I/II open-label trials reported preserved or improved visual acuity in approximately 60-70% of treated eyes at two years; Phase III data have been more equivocal, and no regulatory approval had been granted as of 2025.

medicalTreatments.name: Anti-VEGF intravitreal injections for choroidal neovascularisation
medicalTreatments.description: Ranibizumab, aflibercept, or bevacizumab injected into the vitreous suppress abnormal choroidal new vessel growth, which can complicate end-stage choroideremia and cause rapid central visual loss if untreated.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Anti-VEGF therapy stabilises neovascular complications in approximately 80-90% of cases in the short term, consistent with outcomes in other neovascular conditions.

medicalTreatments.name: Cataract surgery with appropriate prognosis counselling
medicalTreatments.description: Posterior subcapsular cataracts are more frequent in choroideremia than in age-matched individuals, possibly related to choroidal circulation changes and light scatter; phacoemulsification can restore lenticular optical clarity, though the benefit is limited by the extent of underlying retinal degeneration.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Visual acuity improvement from cataract surgery in choroideremia depends on the degree of central retinal preservation; patients with intact foveal ellipsoid zones may gain 2-4 Snellen lines.

medicalTreatments.name: Low vision aids and visual rehabilitation
medicalTreatments.description: Eccentric viewing training, magnification devices, screen readers, and orientation and mobility training help individuals maximise their remaining functional vision as peripheral and then central vision declines progressively.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Structured low vision rehabilitation improves reading speed and daily living task performance by approximately 30-60% in individuals with central visual field loss across inherited retinal dystrophies.

surgicalOptions.name: Subretinal injection for gene therapy delivery
surgicalOptions.description: A vitreoretinal surgical procedure under anaesthesia in which a small gauge cannula is passed through the retina to inject the viral vector into the subretinal space, creating a temporary bleb over the target photoreceptors and RPE. Risks include transient retinal detachment, cataract formation, and subretinal haemorrhage.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Surgical delivery is technically successful in over 90% of trial procedures; visual outcomes depend on the stage of retinal degeneration at the time of treatment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Nutritional support and antioxidant supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Vitamins C and E, lutein, zeaxanthin, and omega-3 fatty acids are widely consumed by people with inherited retinal dystrophies; evidence from choroideremia-specific trials is absent, but the antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties are plausible and the safety profile is acceptable.

alternativeTreatments.name: Patient registry participation and clinical trial enrolment
alternativeTreatments.description: Registering with national and international choroideremia patient registries (such as those coordinated by the Choroideremia Research Foundation UK and the Foundation Fighting Blindness) connects individuals with trial opportunities and ensures they receive information about new treatment advances as they become available.

whySeeSpecialist: All individuals with suspected choroideremia, including those with night blindness and a relevant family history, should be referred promptly to a retinal specialist at a centre with expertise in inherited retinal dystrophies. Specialist involvement is essential for establishing a genetic diagnosis (required for trial eligibility and accurate genetic counselling), performing multimodal imaging and ERG to characterise disease stage, monitoring progression with the sensitive outcome measures used in clinical trials, and referring patients to active gene therapy trials. Carrier female relatives of affected males should also be assessed and offered genetic counselling and reproductive options including preimplantation genetic testing.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a vitreoretinal or retinal specialist ophthalmologist at a tertiary centre with a dedicated inherited retinal dystrophy clinic. In the UK, NHS specialised commissioning funds inherited retinal dystrophy services at designated centres. In the US, Foundation Fighting Blindness accredited clinical trial centres can be found via the clinical trial finder at fightingblindness.org. The retinal team should have access to full-field ERG, high-resolution OCT, fundus autofluorescence, microperimetry, and — ideally — active participation in CHM gene therapy trials. Ask whether they collaborate with a clinical geneticist for genetic counselling and cascade testing.
preventionStrategies: For families with a known CHM mutation, genetic counselling before or during pregnancy allows consideration of preimplantation genetic testing (PGT) to avoid transmission to male offspring.
Carrier female relatives of an affected male should be offered carrier testing to understand their risk and their children's risk accurately.
Protect remaining photoreceptors from additional insults: wear UV-blocking sunglasses outdoors, avoid smoking (which reduces retinal vascular perfusion), and ensure adequate nutrition.

Register with the Choroideremia Research Foundation or equivalent national patient registry to stay informed about gene therapy trials; earlier-stage disease is likely to benefit most from any gene replacement therapy.
Report any acute visual deterioration promptly to the retinal specialist — choroidal neovascularisation is a treatable complication that, if missed, can cause rapid irreversible central visual loss.
lifestyleModifications: Use optimal lighting at home — bright directional lighting for reading and task work, night-lights in corridors and bathrooms to prevent falls as night vision fails.
Wear UV-absorbing lenses outdoors to reduce photochemical load on surviving photoreceptors.
Inform the DVLA (UK) or equivalent licensing authority when driving becomes unsafe; legal obligations apply and a formal visual field assessment is usually required.
Use large-print or electronic magnification for reading as visual acuity declines, and explore screen reader and voice-control technology for computers and smartphones.

Consider proactive orientation and mobility training with a specialist rehabilitation officer while vision is still relatively preserved — early training is more effective than waiting until vision is severely restricted.
Join patient support networks such as the Choroideremia Research Foundation or Foundation Fighting Blindness for peer support, current trial information, and practical living resources.

recommended: Oily fish (salmon, mackerel, sardines) and walnuts as sources of omega-3 fatty acids supporting retinal membrane integrity
Lutein and zeaxanthin from kale, spinach, and eggs — macular carotenoids that may reduce oxidative photoreceptor damage
Antioxidant-rich fruit and vegetables (berries, citrus, bell peppers) to reduce systemic and retinal oxidative stress
Vitamin A from sweet potato, liver, and dairy — essential for rod photoreceptor outer segment renewal
avoid: Smoking — nicotine and carbon monoxide impair choroidal blood flow and accelerate photoreceptor damage
Excessive alcohol, which depletes retinal protective micronutrients and impairs dark adaptation
Diets severely lacking in antioxidants, particularly relevant as the remaining photoreceptors are under chronic oxidative stress

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate physical exercise is encouraged; there are no choroideremia-specific exercise restrictions. Aerobic exercise improves retinal and choroidal vascular perfusion, which may offer marginal protective benefit to surviving photoreceptors. As peripheral vision narrows, supervised or guided activities in environments with good lighting are preferable to high-speed sports or driving. Orientation and mobility training with a certified specialist can maintain safe community independence as peripheral field loss progresses.
dailyManagement: Keep regular retinal monitoring appointments (typically every 12 months or more often in trial participants) for OCT, FAF, and microperimetry to track the preserved ellipsoid zone area.
Maintain an updated list of your CHM mutation, ERG findings, and current central visual field area for rapid trial eligibility assessment if a new gene therapy trial opens.
Use a walking cane or high-visibility vest in unfamiliar or crowded environments when peripheral field loss becomes significant.
Inform your ophthalmologist immediately if you notice any sudden change in central vision — choroidal neovascularisation requires prompt anti-VEGF treatment.
Contact your local authority low vision service or equivalent rehabilitation provider as vision begins to affect daily tasks, rather than waiting until vision is severely restricted.
Keep the Choroideremia Research Foundation or Foundation Fighting Blindness trial finder bookmarked and check it annually for new trial openings.
prognosis: Choroideremia follows a slowly progressive but ultimately severe course in affected males. Night blindness and peripheral field loss typically begin in the first two decades and progress over the next three to four decades before central vision is substantially impaired. Most affected males retain reading vision into their fourth or fifth decade, and some preserve useful central vision into the sixth or even seventh decade, particularly those with hypomorphic CHM variants. However, the natural history leads to legal blindness (visual acuity worse than 6/60 or visual field less than 20 degrees) in the majority of males by age 50-60. Female carriers have an excellent prognosis; the approximately 5% who develop meaningful visual loss typically do so slowly and rarely reach legal blindness. The advent of gene therapy clinical trials has transformed the outlook, with the reasonable possibility that an approved treatment will be available within the next decade, making early genetic diagnosis and registration with an inherited retinal dystrophy centre more important than ever.
recoveryTimeline: Choroideremia does not have a recovery timeline — the degeneration is progressive and the lost cells do not regenerate with current treatments. In gene therapy trial participants who respond, preservation of the ellipsoid zone area and stabilisation of visual sensitivity are the primary goals, with measurable outcomes assessed at 12 and 24 months post-treatment. Anti-VEGF treatment for choroidal neovascularisation stabilises vision within four to six weeks of initiation. Cataract surgery provides visual improvement within days to weeks, with final acuity reached at approximately six weeks postoperatively.
complications: Legal blindness by the fifth to sixth decade in most affected males — progressive concentric visual field loss leads to severe restriction of daily function, employment capacity, and independent mobility.

Choroidal neovascular membrane — develops in a minority of patients with advanced disease, causing rapid central visual deterioration; recognisable by sudden reduction in central acuity and treatable with anti-VEGF injections.
Posterior subcapsular cataract — more common than in the age-matched general population, reducing optical clarity and worsening reading ability in already visually impaired individuals.
Falls and injury from peripheral field loss — as the visual field contracts below 60-70 degrees, navigation in unfamiliar environments becomes hazardous; formal mobility assessment is important.

Psychological impact and depression — the progressive nature of the disease and its onset in childhood create significant anxiety and depressive symptoms; access to psychological support and peer networks is a recognised part of holistic care.
Retinal detachment — an uncommon complication in very advanced disease where choroidal atrophy may compromise the structural support of the peripheral retina.

supportResources.name: Choroideremia Research Foundation UK
supportResources.description: UK-based patient organisation providing information on the condition, clinical trials, genetic counselling resources, and patient community support.

supportResources.name: Foundation Fighting Blindness — Choroideremia
supportResources.description: US-based research foundation with a clinical trial finder, research updates, and patient support resources for choroideremia and other inherited retinal dystrophies.

supportResources.name: NHS Inform — Choroideremia
supportResources.description: Patient-facing overview of choroideremia symptoms, diagnosis, and management from NHS Scotland.

confusedWithConditions.name: Choroidal dystrophy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Choroidal dystrophies unrelated to CHM mutations may appear similar on fundoscopy; choroideremia is distinguished by X-linked inheritance, the scalloped peripheral-to-central atrophy pattern, and genetic confirmation of a CHM variant.

confusedWithConditions.name: Retinal pigment epithelium dystrophies
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: RPE dystrophies include multiple entities (Stargardt, Best disease) with varied patterns of foveal or diffuse RPE changes; choroideremia begins peripherally and the molecular basis (CHM gene) is specific and testable.

confusedWithConditions.name: Night blindness
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Night blindness is a symptom, not a diagnosis; vitamin A deficiency is the leading worldwide cause and responds to supplementation, whereas choroideremia causes structural photoreceptor loss not reversible by nutritional treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Choroidal dystrophy
relatedConditions.relevance: A group of conditions sharing choroidal tissue degeneration that is important to distinguish from choroideremia for accurate genetic counselling and eligibility for CHM-specific gene therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Age-related macular degeneration
relatedConditions.relevance: Both conditions involve progressive loss of RPE and photoreceptors; anti-VEGF therapy used in neovascular AMD is also employed for choroidal neovascular complications of choroideremia.

relatedConditions.name: Choroidal degeneration, bilateral
relatedConditions.relevance: Choroideremia produces bilateral choroidal degeneration; this broader category is often the initial ICD-coded finding before the specific genetic diagnosis is confirmed.

relatedConditions.name: Night blindness
relatedConditions.relevance: The first presenting symptom of choroideremia is night blindness, making this the typical entry point for diagnosis and an important condition to evaluate for inherited retinal dystrophy in young males.

faqs.question: What is choroideremia?
faqs.answer: Choroideremia is a rare inherited retinal disease caused by mutations in the CHM gene. It progressively destroys the light-sensitive cells of the retina and the underlying choroid, leading to night blindness, shrinking visual field, and eventual severe visual loss over decades.

faqs.question: Who gets choroideremia?
faqs.answer: Choroideremia almost exclusively affects males because it is caused by a defective gene on the X chromosome. Females who carry the mutation usually have normal or near-normal vision. It affects about 1 in 50,000 people worldwide.

faqs.question: What are the first signs of choroideremia?
faqs.answer: The earliest symptom is difficulty seeing in dim light or darkness, typically appearing in childhood or adolescence. This is followed by slowly narrowing peripheral vision. Central vision used for reading and face recognition is usually preserved into middle age.

faqs.question: Is choroideremia the same as retinitis pigmentosa?
faqs.answer: No, though both are inherited retinal dystrophies causing night blindness and peripheral field loss. Choroideremia is X-linked and caused by the CHM gene, shows a distinctive scalloped choroidal atrophy without bone-spicule pigmentation, and has its own targeted gene therapy. They require separate genetic tests.

faqs.question: Is there a cure for choroideremia?
faqs.answer: There is no approved cure as of 2025. Gene therapy trials using AAV2 vectors carrying a corrected copy of CHM are in advanced clinical stages and have shown promising safety data; several Phase III trials are ongoing. Early diagnosis and registration with a trial centre are advisable.

faqs.question: How fast does choroideremia progress?
faqs.answer: Choroideremia progresses slowly over decades. Most affected males retain useful central vision for reading into their 40s or 50s, but eventually reach legal blindness, often between ages 50 and 65. The rate of progression varies between individuals and is influenced by the specific CHM mutation.

faqs.question: Can women get choroideremia?
faqs.answer: Female carriers rarely develop significant visual impairment. About 5% of female carriers have detectable peripheral visual field loss, but severe visual impairment from choroideremia is extremely uncommon in females. All daughters of an affected male are obligate carriers.

faqs.question: How is choroideremia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines dilated fundus examination, optical coherence tomography, fundus autofluorescence, electroretinography, and genetic testing of the CHM gene. The characteristic scalloped peripheral atrophy on fundoscopy with preserved central vision is highly distinctive.

faqs.question: Is choroideremia hereditary?
faqs.answer: Yes. Choroideremia is inherited in an X-linked recessive pattern. An affected male's daughters are all carriers; his sons are unaffected. A carrier female has a 50% chance of passing the mutation to each son (who will be affected) and a 50% chance to each daughter (who will be a carrier).

faqs.question: What gene therapy is available for choroideremia?
faqs.answer: Adeno-associated virus type 2 (AAV2) carrying a functional CHM gene is injected under the retina in a surgical procedure. Early clinical trials showed preservation of vision in some patients. Phase III trials were ongoing as of 2025 and no product was approved yet, but this is the most advanced treatment pipeline for the condition.

faqs.question: Can choroideremia lead to total blindness?
faqs.answer: Yes. In most affected males, choroideremia progresses to legal blindness — defined as visual acuity worse than 6/60 or a visual field of less than 20 degrees — typically between ages 50 and 65. Total loss of all light perception is less common and occurs later.

faqs.question: What should I do if my son has night blindness?
faqs.answer: Any boy with persistent difficulty seeing in dim light should have a dilated fundus examination by an ophthalmologist. If there is a family history of X-linked visual impairment, early genetic testing for CHM mutations is advisable. Early diagnosis is important for trial eligibility and genetic counselling.

faqs.question: How is choroideremia different from age-related macular degeneration?
faqs.answer: Choroideremia is a genetic disease beginning in the retinal periphery that affects mainly young and middle-aged males. AMD affects the central macula in older adults of both sexes and is driven by aging, UV exposure, and cardiovascular risk factors. The visual loss pattern and genetics are completely different.

faqs.question: Should I wear sunglasses if I have choroideremia?
faqs.answer: Yes. UV-blocking sunglasses are recommended to reduce photochemical oxidative stress on surviving photoreceptors. Blue light blocking lenses may also reduce glare, which is a common symptom as the disease progresses.

faqs.question: What support is available for people with choroideremia?
faqs.answer: The Choroideremia Research Foundation UK and the Foundation Fighting Blindness in the US offer patient registries, trial information, and peer support. NHS low vision rehabilitation services and social care mobility training are available in the UK for people with significant vision loss.

faqs.question: Can choroideremia affect children?
faqs.answer: Yes. Night blindness typically begins in childhood or adolescence, often in the first decade. The peripheral visual field begins to narrow in the teens and twenties. It is important to diagnose the condition in childhood to enable appropriate school and activity adjustments and register for future trials.

faqs.question: What happens at a choroideremia eye appointment?
faqs.answer: Appointments typically include vision and colour vision testing, pupil dilation, dilated fundus examination, OCT scanning of the macula, visual field testing, and sometimes electroretinography. Regular appointments every 6-12 months track the preserved ellipsoid zone area and any complications.

faqs.question: Can diet or supplements slow choroideremia?
faqs.answer: No supplement has been proven to slow choroideremia progression in clinical trials. Lutein, zeaxanthin, vitamin C and E, and omega-3 fatty acids are widely used because of their established retinal protective role in other conditions, and their safety profile is acceptable.

faqs.question: Is choroideremia painful?
faqs.answer: Choroideremia is not painful. It causes progressive visual loss without any discomfort in the eye itself. Rare complications such as choroidal neovascular membranes may occasionally cause mild distortion or blur but not significant pain.

faqs.question: How can I find a choroideremia gene therapy trial?
faqs.answer: The Foundation Fighting Blindness clinical trial finder at fightingblindness.org and clinicaltrials.gov list all open choroideremia trials. Your retinal specialist at an inherited retinal dystrophy centre can assess your eligibility based on your CHM mutation, visual acuity, and preserved ellipsoid zone area.

metaDescription: Choroideremia is an X-linked inherited retinal dystrophy causing night blindness and progressive vision loss in males. Learn diagnosis and gene therapy options.
